steps.title: Identify the signal that would test the claim
steps.content: The researchers claim awe inspires altruistic behavior. The study tests this with an awe condition (tall trees) and a control (nondescript building), measuring whether participants help pick up spilled pens. The key signal is whether the awe-exposed group shows more helping.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
Choice A shows the predicted pattern: tree-gazers helped significantly more, directly confirming that awe prompted altruism. This is correct.

Choice B measures the language participants used in a postexperiment survey. That tells us how they described their feelings, not whether awe caused the helping behavior — it confuses the direction of the question.

Choice C shows that non-helpers reported awe regardless of condition. This would actually contradict the claim: if people who felt awe still did not help, awe is not reliably linked to altruism.

Choice D measures whether participants noticed the dropped pens. Noticing is a matter of attention, not altruism — it does not show that awe increased helping.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
